sentences.text: The chicken coop.
He was underneath the chicken coop.
At night, when the family was discussing this, so proud of themselves that they did such a marvelous thing.
They helped the Germans.
They killed another ten Jews.
He came out.
He said, this won't be forgotten.
It will come the day that you'll be smashed the same way.
Then some of his sons came with a hack-- a sword-- get him over the head.
Took him out right away.
The farmer's son got up and killed the last boy that was hiding.
How do I know this story?
When the-- they didn't show up for a while, more than the time that was assigned for them, intelligence from our brigade was looking for them.
And you know, there is no secret.
So somebody probably put them in that direction.
And then my husband sent out his brother, which was in charge, always, of the fighting group, [? assail, ?] and he put him-- he told them not a thing should be touched in this house.
Not a thing, no matter how tempting it is, because usually they took a blanket, they took a scarf, they took a warm coat, whatever they found to take.
So he said, not a thing.
Everything should go with fire with them together.
I should be put in-- put out a sign.
That's what's happening to people that are trying to kill-- keep clothes for the Germans, trying to-- to help them, and get rid of Jews.
And that will happen to anybody else who will do like this.
But one guy-- I just saw him a week ago-- could not resist.
He saw a beautiful jacket, and he took it.
He asked.
He asked the commander that was with them if I can take it.
He said, go ahead and take it.
And in the jacket, in the back of his jacket, in the inside pocket, he found a letter-- a German letter-- which was placed in Ro-- in Minsk when they started, you know, after the war.
The SA document said-- big letter from the Germans-- thank you so much for helping us out to-- to kill the Jews, to find them, and for sending your son to-- to the place-- to the right place.
And you'll be awarded for it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They award anybody 400,000 ruble for-- to catch my husband, to-- to catch him or to kill him or to bring him alive.
The Germans.
Right.
It started slowly-- 300, 400, 750.
Then we had to go to a very big-- the Germans made a big-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Raid on us.
And we were ten days in-- in water.
And everybody survived-- small kids.
This was already in [INAUDIBLE] This was already in the deep woods of the labor camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In dumps and water.
No food, no nothing.
And every-- not one person was missing.
When the-- when the group was already so big, it was established-- my husband had a choice-- I told you.
To stay with the older and young, and children, or to leave them and-- and go on his own.
He never accepted it, no matter how.
And believe me, there was plenty problems.
As if-- as today, there's different problems in life.
It was problems because our Jews cannot sit still.
Like I mentioned, they wanted-- everybody wanted to be in the command.
Commander can be the only one.
And they find different things.
They found different things to tell and to go to the Russian-- there was a-- my husband wasn't the commander of the partisans.
He was a commander of his brigade, but he has the head bosses over him that he had to listen to and obey.
Sent from-- from Russia that they had their main headquarters.
And the planes from Russia used to come there.
No.
No.
That was the Russians.
The Russian Army.
Yes.
Platon.
He was the whole boss of ours.
Yes.

And our Jews weren't lazy to go and complain, and say that he's not a communist, that he's not having the communist party the right way At that time, in order-- you had to be a-- you know, to-- they should like you.
They should-- they should be good friends with you.
And they tried from all sides.
There were plenty of complaints from the inside people to the main management.
Tuvia stayed.
We-- we went out, 1,250 people.
He wasn't [INAUDIBLE].
You couldn't behave with the family camp without fighters.
They took-- the Russians tried to, not once, to take away from us, to divide us, to take away some fighters from us.
But he built it again.
He did it again.
And nobody was willing-- very little were willing to leave the camp.
They wanted to stay with him.
Yes.
Yes.
One camp.
And workshops, one camp, a bakery, a salami factory, a soap factory, a-- no.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They used to-- no, like a bath, but they took--
A steam bath.
They used to take turns, you know.
There was plenty of flies, plenty of in-- of insects.
It's woods.
And each one had his own turn, and when the time came, he had to go and take care in it.
There were doctors.
There was a hospital.
There were shoemakers.
There were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every little thing what a normal life needs, there were there.
And the-- more the sen-- the seniority was more occupied with that.
Yeah.
Sure.
Whatever Tuvia-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some were volunteers that they know how to do this, a watchmaker or this, or even for the rifles, for the guns.
You needed-- you had to clean, you had something that breaks, something-- everything was organized.
People from before the war knew some work, and that's where they volunteered.
I had-- I was-- at that time, I was already with Tuvia.
So a lot of-- from other-- from other brigades came always visiting.
We had a kindergarten.
We had shows.
We had-- we had-- we had to receive them.
We had to, you know, to host them.
And I was staying on guard, too.
And when I'd see my husband in the mornings, he said, Mr.
So-and-So, Mr.
Commander, let me tell you that the night was quiet.
Nobody showed up.
Nobody-- no shooting, no this, no that.
I wasn't [INAUDIBLE].
I never played the role of a Miss Commander-- Mrs.
Commander.
Never.
Whoever came from outside, I tried always to host them, to give them, to help them.
But you know, you can not be [INAUDIBLE].
They'll find anyway something to say on you.
And once you're in commend, there is always something to say because you cannot satisfy the world.
Very well.
We had children that they wanted-- that-- the parents didn't want to send them.
But they were-- they wanted-- the Russian people wanted to have them in Russia, and to go on, you know, with their lives, because they were very talented in singing and dancing.
We had a kindergarten with a teacher.
Who was the teacher?
Sechel. ?] She was-- she was from the Polish region that came in '39 and escaped.
And she came to Lida, and then she came to the woods, and-- How-- did the children obey the discipline as well?
Very well.
In fact, when we were raided, and children-- they were sitting in water without food.
They just said that's, I think, from God, because not a boo, not a cry.
We had probably about 10 children like this.
Children were born in the woods too.
We helped.
We had quite a few doctors.
In fact, other brigades used to come to our doctors.
There were marriages before the war, and there were marriages, you know, that [INAUDIBLE] or a woman-- or a wife was killed or a husband was killed, or when children were killed.
The main thing were the children, because they didn't leave too many children alive, only the ones that escaped.
That's was their aim-- the German's aim, to kill the children.
That was their pleasure.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some were very lonely.
They lost everybody.
They were just alone.
And the circumstances weren't too good.
Individually, Tuvia couldn't take care on everybody, individually.

So if you had a man around you, he was worried about you-- to bring you the food, to bring you a pair of socks, or a pair of-- of shoes or whatever he saw that he could grab from a pa-- from a farm or from a village.
You were more secured having somebody at the site.
But not everybody could do this again too.
Some of them had a few.
Some of them didn't have nobody.
That's-- that was their own decision.
Some of them didn't want to, they-- as they came, so they left.
They tried.
Not too many, no.
No.
I used to go with Tuvia to, let's say, when he had to go places, but-- you know, to meet somebody or some-- some special place.
There were Russian holidays, special holidays, they used to observe, and we had to observe too.
We had shows that the Russian came to us.
You had to host them.
The kids performed.
But-- but to say that I was a fighter?
No.
I wasn't a fighter.
I tried it a few times, but I tried not to do that.
Just my teaching.
If you ask them today, they'll sure did.
But I don't-- I don't think so.
No.
Because there wasn't.
That's why I cannot remember that.
This and they-- they-- some of them worked in the kitchen.
Some of them worked in the bakery.
Some of them worked in the-- in the-- where they had the-- it was a long-- a long, special building, made for-- for the shoemakers, for the dressmakers, for the-- for the barbers, for everybody.
It was like a, you know, a flea market, that everybody had a station where he worked.
A lot of people worked, and a lot of people did not.
I'm telling you.
It just came to my mind.
It looked like a flea market, booths by booths by booths, and there was--
Like they do barracks.
It was a big barrack.
A long--
Yeah, when we leaved, but this was on top, because we were already in the deeper woods.
So this was on top.
The living quarters were there too, on top, in the-- in the deep woods in the [INAUDIBLE].
It depends from who-- who built what.
That's-- the people themselves had to build.
Whatever the-- some did it from wood.
Some did it from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from-- you know, they had to be covered and look like the trees on top.
Camouflage.
Or in the beginning, when we started, if you walked out and there was snow, you had the strainer and you covered with the snow-- the-- the snow bed.
The steps.
Footsteps.
Yeah.
Nothing fancy.
Believe me, nothing fancy.
It was structured from wood, from the wood that they had in the woods.
They couldn't structure from anything else.
From logs.
For us, Tuvia didn't build it.
There were people that built it.
But each one, that if he wanted nicer quarters or nicer place to live, they used to build for themselves.
And the beds they built too, from the same wood logs.
Well that's the first that we used to find-- is water wells.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, to--
Dig.
Dig.
Dig.
Medical care-- many people especially went-- like I mentioned [?
Kozlowski, ?] in the beginning, used to bring.
But later on, the people that came from the ghettos and from all over.
It wasn't a supply from all kind of antibiotics, that's for sure.
In very serious cases, we used-- we used sulfa at that time.
Sulfa.
The one that came from the ghettos.
The fresh ones that came from the ghettos, or the doctors that came to our-- to us.
They had it supplies.
They stealed from the Germans and they brought along.
Some people had pills, too, that they said, if they'll fall alive in the hands of the Germans, they want to kill themselves.
They want to take a pill and go.
Right.
No.
It wasn't so bad in the woods.
We had a hospital with nurses and doctors.
In the camp, aside.
In case there's a raid on the camp, there should be aside.
First of all, Tuvia had a radio, which was given to him from one of his, you know, intelligence-- from the-- not Jewish intelligence, but from his friends from once upon a time, that he had them.
Second of all, they-- they used== the farmers used to go to the-- to the cities [INAUDIBLE] bring newspaper.
Not every day, but more or less, we knew what's going on.
You mean if-- we had some very intellectuals.
We had a professor that kept a diary.
But then he was arrested and everything was taken from him, by crossing the border of Poland to Czechoslovakia.
That's right.
Dr.
Amarat.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] His wife is alive.
Very much so.
We knew every holiday.
And there was a special group from older people that observed, who had the shochet.
You know what a shochet means?
I'll explain to you.
How do you say a shochet in--
Slaughterer.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He had- he-- in fact, he has a few daughters, and when he used to come with Tuvia, all five daughters were waiting for us at the airport.
Because they came-- five daughters and a husband-- a father and a mother-- to the woods.
So there were seven people survived form [?
Veranova. ?] And he was like the-- the rabbi of all the religious people.
So one time-- I mentioned before Platon-- he was the boss of all the partisans, and he ruled, the General Platon.
And he came-- he decided one time, he had so much complaints about that they were eating, were not doing nothing, that Tuvia does this, that [?
Tuvia does ?] that.
And he called Tuvia one time and another time, and he tried to explain to him that it's not so.
So he said, you know what-- he said to him, you know what?
You come to the base and take a look for yourself.
And-- and then you maybe all your answer-- questions will be answered.
And he did.
It took a while, but he did.
Oh, we received him beautifully.
He went-- he couldn't get over it.
He couldn't get over the kids.
He couldn't get over the-- the workshops.
He couldn't get over nothing.
[INAUDIBLE] in camp where leather was in a big pail.
What did they-- what did they use the leather for?
Repairing shoes.
So they used to put the leather in water, and I don't know how they did it.
But it was-- at the-- and they-- they had a lot of use of the leather, what they-- sometimes they used to make a pair of boots, a pair of shoes.
And this was a very big barrack for the leather.
It was pretty smelly because leather smells.
But there, they used to come, for [NON-ENGLISH], for the holidays.
And the shochet was on top of them all.
And sure enough, Platon walked in there and looked and wondered how-- how are they making the leather, how are this.
He couldn't-- he couldn't believe his eyes.
He said, what are you doing.
Those people that are saying and-- and all the time [NON-ENGLISH]?
So Tuvia was afraid to tell him that they are davening.
So he said to him, you know, they're all learning the-- the Bible of the communism and the-- the Marxism and all that.
Oh, he couldn't-- you know, I can't believe it.
Yeah, he says.
We have all kind of groups, that each one is learning something else.
And so today, they-- all the people have nothing to do so they are learning the story of the communists.
And he believed it.
I have no idea.
But was a time, I-- I-- it's very-- if it's important, I'll find out, it's-- because I have somebody to find out.
But he-- there were seven people there.
His five daughters are still in Israel.
We knew when it's Pesach.
We could-- we knew-- we baked matzohs.
No.
[INAUDIBLE] But they-- this group from the-- this-- they baked matzohs.
They marked it, the holidays.
Listen, we couldn't do these things because we didn't know when we had to run.
It wasn't that I'm sitting in the house and if it's raining I don't-- there was nowhere I-- they didn't-- no rain, and no snow, and no wind, and no nothing.
You want to survive, so you go against everything.
You run, you go, you-- you fall, you get up, you go again.
Not me.
Me?
Yeah.
I'm a, by nature, a pessimist.
That's what they say, or-- I don't feel like this, but that's what they all.
But Tuvia always was optimistic.
Not everybody, but some of us.
Cliques?
There were cliques.
One city didn't bother with the other.
You know, everybody-- you must understand now that everybody was afraid, so much.
They wanted so much to survive, that they thought, if another person-- there were plenty of people that were against it.
We came, the first ones to the woods.
That they were saying, what will we do with food?
How will we feed them?
That time, it wasn't organized.
When they chose my husband as a commander, it was already the seven people that I mentioned.

And then they went into a meeting and they chose him as a commander because he had military knowledge, he served in the Polish Army, he was good in languages, he was good in speech, and he was good in organizing.
And he was the oldest.
So they-- they choosed him.
But-- so each one that came from the ghetto, they were afraid.
Maybe he will take away a piece from me.
Maybe he will take away a piece from me.
It was-- they didn't click together.
I didn't say they didn't trust.
They were afraid that-- that they'll take away from him something.
Maybe it will be my-- maybe we'll get less soup by-- by some other people coming than-- than he used to, you know?
It was a-- it was a kitchen for everybody.
It was a kitchen for the-- for the special milk for the children, more.
But for the elderly, he tried to pay more attention, you know, like, to give them a bigger supply.
But they were afraid that each one-- that they'll take away from me something.
Maybe it won't be enough for me.
And people are funny.
They're-- if you want to live, why shouldn't I now want to live?
So how did Tuvia cope with that?
Very, very hard.
With other Jewish people, it's not easy to go because we have our ideas.
We have our mindset.
And very-- it's very hard sometimes to-- to cope with them.
Not-- very-- they gave him a lot of trouble.
Somebody else in his place, not with his strong nature and with his disciplined hand, we'd never survive with so many people.
Oh, we had a-- was sent to jail.
We had a jail, too.
Two people were killed for disobeying intentionally.
No.
It was a trial, actually.
It was a trial that was a decision.
WE had somebody like a prosecutor always, that he finished law in-- in Europe.
And he was the one to make the decisions.
They wanted to make a group into-- in the same group to go against Tuvia.
To break up.
The one that worked on it.
He was in jail.
He didn't quiet down.
He did his-- what he felt to do.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he didn't go with it.
Tuvia was-- had nothing to do with it.
There were people that felt the-- their-- whatever was coming to them.
Listen.
One is not 1,250.
To destroy this much was-- was unnecessary.
